checks.a_analysis: The output captures the text from the top legend and the bottom metadata table accurately. The title 'Table Seal Imagery Diagram 1468' is an expected AI-generated addition for accessibility and not a fabrication of document content. The SSID descriptions and table contents all have a clear basis in the source document.
No contact information such as phone numbers, emails, or URLs is present in the source or the output.

The document consists of a single page. While the central diagram's text labels are missing from the text elements (covered under C5), the diagram itself is represented as a series of image objects. The main structural components (legend, diagram area, and metadata table) are all accounted for in the reading order.
The source document contains one table at the bottom with two rows of project metadata. The output JSON contains one table with two rows. The cell contents and layout are faithfully preserved.
checks.b_evidence_source: Table at bottom of page with 2 rows (Date/Project/Created By and Department/Description).
checks.b_evidence_output: Table element at reading_order 2 with 2 rows containing corresponding text.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: A very large amount of text content located within the central diagram and the 'SSIDs' and 'VLANs & Subnets' list boxes has been entirely omitted as text elements. While the diagram is provided as images, the textual information contained within it—which constitutes more than 25% of the total text on the page—is not extracted into the semantic JSON structure. This includes network labels, status descriptions, and VLAN assignments.
checks.b_evidence_source: Text labels: 'Guests', 'State Employees', 'Agency Networks', 'Internet Access', 'State Network', 'Employee – hidden', 'NCGovRoam – broadcast', 'NCGov Guest – broadcast', 'Access Point', 'XIQ', 'Data Center', 'Enterprise AD', 'NCID', 'Policy Servers (EDC & WDC)', 'Employee – 210, agency', 'NCGovRoam – 410, guest', 'NCGov Guest – 410, guest', etc.
checks.b_evidence_output: The JSON output completely lacks these text strings in its text or list elements, representing the diagram only via image objects.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text that was successfully extracted (legend and table) is accurate and matches the source document exactly.

checks.a_analysis: The output document contains massive duplication of visual information. The main diagram is captured in its entirety as an image in element index 2 (reading_order 3). Subsequently, the pipeline has extracted nearly 50 individual snippets of the same diagram (icons, text blocks, and small areas) as separate image elements (element indices 3 through 51). This results in the same information being presented multiple times in the structured output.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source contains one integrated diagram containing various icons, text, and labels.
checks.b_evidence_output: The output contains a main image (index 2) and 49 additional image elements (indices 3-51) which are cropped segments of the content already present in the main image.

checks.a_analysis: The output document does not utilize any heading tags (H1-H6). While the source document has visual headings within the diagram (e.g., 'SSIDs', 'VLANs & Subnets'), these have been captured as images rather than structural text headings. Since no headings exist in the output, there is no hierarchy to evaluate for 'breaks' (skipping levels), although the lack of structure is a fidelity issue.

The footer table at the bottom of the document has been correctly identified and structurally mapped. The source table has three columns in the first row and two in the second row (with a wide cell for 'Description'). The JSON output accurately reflects this row and cell count.
checks.b_evidence_source: Footer table with 'Date: April 1 2025', 'Project: NCGovRoam', etc.
checks.b_evidence_output: The 'table' element in element index 1 contains 2 rows. Row 1 has 3 cells; Row 2 has 2 cells.

checks.a_analysis: The reading order of the document is materially incorrect. In the source document, the logical flow is the SSID description list at the top, the network diagram in the center, and the administrative metadata table at the very bottom. The output JSON places the footer table (reading_order 2) before the network diagram (reading_order 3), disrupting the logical top-to-bottom flow of the document.
checks.b_evidence_source: The administrative table (Date, Project, etc.) is located at the bottom of the page.
checks.b_evidence_output: The table element (element index 1) is assigned 'reading_order': 2, while the main diagram image (element index 2) is assigned 'reading_order': 3.

checks.a_analysis: The only list converted as text is the SSID description list at the top of the page. This list is correctly identified as an unordered list and its three items are preserved accurately. Lists embedded within the diagram are not captured as list structures but as images, which is consistent with image-based capture for those regions.
checks.b_evidence_source: List at top: 'Employee SSID...', 'NCGovRoam SSID...', 'NCGov Guest SSID...'
checks.b_evidence_output: An 'unordered' list element with three items corresponding to the source text.

checks.a_analysis: There are no links or URLs present in either the source PDF or the output JSON.

checks.a_analysis: The source PDF features a highly structured network diagram where spatial positioning, grouping, and directional arrows are critical to understanding the information flow. In the HTML output, this visual organization is completely discarded. The diagram is decomposed into a linear vertical stack of text followed by isolated image fragments. This transformation from a two-dimensional schematic to a one-dimensional list destroys the intended logic and visual flow of the document.
checks.b_evidence_source: A cohesive network diagram on page 1 showing relationships between users, access points, and various networks (Internet, Agency, State) using a specific spatial layout and arrows.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML output presents the content as a vertical sequence: SSID text, then a metadata table, then a series of fragmented image slices (green/red blobs) stacked vertically without the original spatial context.
checks.b_evidence_location: Main body of the document, following the SSID text list.

checks.a_analysis: The metadata table at the bottom of the source PDF is correctly identified and rendered as an HTML table. Although the styling (background colors and exact cell widths) differs, the logical structure of rows and columns is preserved, and the content remains legible and associated correctly with its headers.

The central meaningful image in the document—the network diagram—is broken. Instead of a single coherent graphic or an accessible reconstruction, the output provides a series of cropped, blurry image fragments (mostly showing the gradient edges of the 'cloud' elements). Critical meaningful components such as the laptop/user icons, server icons, and the directional arrows that define the network path are missing or rendered as unidentifiable slices.
checks.b_evidence_source: Complex diagram containing icons for 'State Employees', 'Guests', 'Access Points', and 'Servers' connected by blue and red dashed arrows.
checks.b_evidence_output: A series of approximately 20 individual image fragments showing only blurry green and red gradients, missing the icons and connective arrows that make the diagram meaningful.
checks.b_evidence_location: Lower half of the HTML document screenshot.

checks.a_analysis: There is no evidence of body content duplication. Each unique piece of text and each image fragment (though broken) appears only once in the body of the converted document.
